1: Psalm 70 is virtually identical to Ps 40:13-17, condensed into a standalone emergency petition. The superscription adds לְהַזְכִּיר (lĕhazzkîr, "to bring to remembrance") — the hiphil infinitive of zākar: to remember. This marks it as a liturgical piece for "bringing [the worshiper's situation] to remembrance" before God (as in the ʾazkārāh, the memorial portion of the grain offering — Lev 2:2). The superscription frames the whole psalm as an act of presenting an urgent case to the divine court. The compression relative to Ps 40:13-17 (where the parallel passage follows extended thanksgiving) signals a standalone emergency use: extracted from its longer context and deployed as an urgent cry on its own. אֱלֹהִים לְהַצִּילֵנִי יְהוָה לְעֶזְרָתִי חוּשָׁה (ʾĕlōhîm lĕhaṣṣîlênî yĕhwāh lĕʿezrātî ḥûshāh, "O God, hurry to deliver me! O LORD, hurry to help me!") — two divine names paired with the single urgent command ḥûshāh (hurry! from ḥûsh: to hasten, to move quickly): the cry for speed from the one who cannot wait.
2: יֵבֹשׁוּ וְיַחְפְּרוּ מְבַקְשֵׁי נַפְשִׁי (yēbōshû wĕyaḥpĕrû mĕbaqshê napshî, "Let those who seek my life be put to shame and confusion") — bôsh: to be put to shame; ḥāpar: to be confounded, to be humiliated (to dig or scratch the ground in embarrassment — the image of the defeated person unable to meet anyone's gaze). יִסֹּגוּ אָחוֹר וְיִכָּלְמוּ חֲפֵצֵי רָעָתִי (yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikkālĕmû ḥăpêṣê rāʿātî, "let those who delight in my harm be turned back and put to disgrace") — sûg: to turn back, to withdraw, to be made to retreat (Isa 42:17: they "shall be turned back in utter shame"); kālam: the public humiliation (Ps 69:6-7). Three words for defeat in sequence: shame (bôsh), confusion (ḥāpar), public disgrace (kālam). The enemies' three-fold humiliation mirrors the Psalmist's three-fold dishonor of Ps 69:19.
3: יָשׁוּבוּ עַל-עֵקֶב בָּשְׁתָּם (yāshûbû ʿal-ʿêqeb bāshtām, "Let them be turned back in their shame") — shûb ʿal-ʿêqeb: "to be turned back on the heel" — the reversal of the direction of march (the army that advanced is turned backward on its own heels). הָאֹמְרִים הֶאָח הֶאָח (hāʾōmĕrîm heʾāḥ heʾāḥ, "those who say, 'Aha! Aha!'") — the mocking exclamation heʾāḥ (as in Ps 35:21: "Aha! Aha! Our eyes have seen it!"; Ezek 26:2: "Aha, the gate of the peoples is broken"). The repeated exclamation captures the gloating of spectators at the Psalmist's downfall. The very exclamation that expressed their delight becomes the occasion of their own reversal.
4: יָשִׂישׂוּ וְיִשְׂמְחוּ בְּךָ כָּל-מְבַקְשֶׁיךָ (yāśîśû wĕyiśmĕḥû bĕkā kol-mĕbaqshêkā, "But let all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you") — the contrast: while the enemies are turned back in shame, those who seek God are turned forward in joy. Śîś and śāmaḥ: the two joy verbs of Ps 68:3 — accumulated delight. וְיֹאמְרוּ תָמִיד יִגְדַּל אֱלֹהִים אֹהֲבֵי יְשׁוּעָתֶךָ (wĕyōʾmĕrû tāmîd yigdal ʾĕlōhîm ʾōhăbê yĕshûʿātekā, "let those who love your salvation say always, 'God be magnified!'") — gādal (qal jussive: let God be made great/magnified): the doxological cry of those who love salvation — not "I am safe" but "God is great." Tāmîd: continually, always (Ps 34:1: "I will bless the LORD at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth").
5: וַאֲנִי עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן (waʾănî ʿānî wĕʾebyôn, "But I am poor and needy") — the contrastive waʾănî (but I) as in Ps 69:29. ʿĀnî (afflicted/humble) and ʾebyôn (destitute/needy): the twin designations of the person who has no earthly resources and therefore turns entirely to God. In Ps 40:17 (the parallel) the same closing verse: the poverty is the qualification for trust, not the obstacle to it. אֱלֹהִים חוּשָׁה-לִּי (ʾĕlōhîm ḥûshāh-llî, "hurry to me, O God!") — the same ḥûshāh as v1: the psalm closes with the same urgent command that opened it. The emergency is maintained through to the final word — no resolution within the psalm itself, only urgent petition. עֶזְרִי וּמְפַלְּטִי אַתָּה (ʿezrî ûmĕpallĕṭî ʾattāh, "You are my help and my deliverer") — ʿēzer: help (the ʿezer of Gen 2:18 — the essential help); pallaṭ (piel participle: the one who causes escape, the liberator — from pālaṭ: to escape). יְהוָה אַל-תְּאַחַר (yĕhwāh ʾal-tĕʾaḥar, "O LORD, do not delay!") — ʾāḥar: to delay, to tarry. The final petition: opened with "hurry" (ḥûshāh), closed with "do not delay" (ʾal-tĕʾaḥar) — the same urgency in different words. The compressed Psalm 70 maintains emergency pitch from first word to last, unlike the extended Ps 40 from which it was drawn.
